h1Title: Hives (Urticaria): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hives, medically termed urticaria, are raised, intensely itchy wheals that appear suddenly on any body surface and typically resolve within 24 hours without leaving a mark. Approximately 20% of the global population experiences at least one episode in their lifetime, and chronic urticaria — defined as recurring wheals for six or more weeks — affects roughly 1% of adults. Individual wheals form when mast cells in the dermis release histamine and other mediators, causing localized plasma extravasation and intense pruritus. Acute hives most often stem from allergic reactions, viral infections, or no identifiable cause. Without treatment, chronic urticaria persists for one to five years in most patients. Second-generation H1 antihistamines control symptoms in up to 80% of cases, and omalizumab resolves refractory chronic urticaria in approximately 65-70% of non-responders.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime prevalence approximately 20% for any episode; chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) affects 0.5-1% of adults at any given time (Magerl et al., EAACI/GA2LEN/EDF/WAO 2022 guidelines)
keyStats.demographics: Chronic urticaria twice as common in women as in men; peak onset age 30-50 years; acute urticaria more common in children and young adults
keyStats.avgAge: Chronic urticaria peaks at age 30-50; acute urticaria can occur at any age; mean duration of chronic spontaneous urticaria 1-5 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 300 million people globally experience chronic urticaria at some point; exact prevalence varies by population and diagnostic criteria

definition:
Urticaria (ICD-10: L50) is a skin condition characterized by wheals, angioedema, or both. A wheal is a superficial skin swelling caused by localized plasma extravasation into the dermis, surrounded by erythema (reflex vasodilation), with associated pruritus or burning lasting less than 24 hours before resolving without scarring. Angioedema — swelling of the deeper dermis, subcutis, or submucosa — accompanies urticaria in approximately 50% of patients with chronic disease and can involve the face, lips, tongue, throat, and extremities, lasting up to 72 hours.

Urticaria is classified by duration and trigger pattern. Acute urticaria lasts under six weeks and resolves in 50% of cases without any treatment; the cause is identified in only about 20-50% of acute episodes. Chronic urticaria persists for more than six weeks and is further divided into chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU), in which wheals occur without a specific external trigger, and chronic inducible urticaria (CIndU), in which wheals are consistently provoked by a physical stimulus — cold, pressure, heat, vibration, water, sunlight, or exercise.

The final common pathway in all forms is mast cell activation in the skin, leading to release of histamine, tryptase, prostaglandin D2, and cytokines that cause wheal formation. In CSU, approximately 35-45% of patients have autoimmune urticaria with functional autoantibodies against IgE or the high-affinity IgE receptor (FcεRIα) on mast cells. Dermatology and allergy-immunology specialists manage this condition jointly.

typesClassification.description: Wheals lasting fewer than 6 weeks. Most common form; often follows an allergic reaction to foods or drugs, or a viral upper respiratory infection. Up to 50% of episodes resolve spontaneously. Cause identified in only 20-50% of cases.

Wheals persisting more than 6 weeks without a consistently identifiable trigger. Associated with autoimmune FcεRIα antibodies in 35-45% of patients. Duration averages 1-5 years; 50% of patients are clear by 12 months.

Wheals reliably triggered by physical stimuli. Subtypes include dermographism (skin stroking), cold urticaria (cold contact), cholinergic urticaria (heat/exercise/sweating), delayed pressure urticaria, solar urticaria, aquagenic urticaria, and vibratory urticaria.

Approximately 50% of chronic urticaria patients also experience angioedema. Most urticaria-associated angioedema is mast-cell-mediated and responds to antihistamines, distinguishing it from bradykinin-mediated hereditary angioedema.

primarySymptoms: Intensely pruritic raised wheals of variable size — from a few millimeters to several centimeters — appearing suddenly on any body surface, with central pallor or erythema.
Individual wheals resolving fully within 24 hours without residual discoloration or scarring — a defining feature distinguishing urticaria from vasculitis or bullous diseases.
Burning or stinging sensation accompanying or replacing pruritus, particularly in cholinergic urticaria triggered by heat or exercise.
Dermographism — firm lines of wheals forming within minutes at sites of skin pressure or scratching, present in approximately 5% of the general population and as a comorbidity in chronic urticaria.
Angioedema of lips, eyelids, hands, feet, or genitalia — asymmetric, non-pitting swelling that may be painful or burning rather than itchy, lasting up to 72 hours.
Flares of wheals coinciding with stress, fever, NSAIDs, alcohol, or tight-fitting clothing in chronic spontaneous urticaria.

Tiny 1-3 mm punctate wheals surrounded by a large erythematous flare within 5-30 minutes of exercise, hot showers, or emotional stress — characteristic of cholinergic urticaria, present in roughly 5-7% of urticaria patients.
Cold-contact urticaria — wheals developing on skin exposed to cold water, cold wind, or ice within minutes, occasionally accompanied by systemic hypotension if large body areas are immersed.
Delayed pressure urticaria — deep painful swelling developing 4-8 hours after sustained pressure on the skin (waistband, shoulder straps); often accompanies CSU.
Sleep disruption from nocturnal pruritus — a significant quality-of-life impact in chronic urticaria affecting 75-80% of patients.
earlyWarningSigns: Wheals appearing consistently within minutes of a specific food, drug, insect sting, or physical trigger — suggests allergic or inducible urticaria requiring allergy evaluation.
Recurrent wheals lasting more than 6 weeks without a clear precipitant — signals chronic spontaneous urticaria needing specialist assessment.
Angioedema of the tongue or throat accompanying urticaria — requires urgent assessment for anaphylaxis, even if episodic.
Individual wheals persisting beyond 24 hours without resolving — raises concern for urticarial vasculitis rather than ordinary urticaria.
emergencySigns: Throat tightness, voice change, stridor, or difficulty swallowing with urticaria — anaphylaxis; administer intramuscular epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg immediately and call emergency services.
Hypotension (systolic below 90 mmHg), tachycardia, and impaired consciousness alongside urticaria — systemic anaphylaxis requiring IV fluids, epinephrine, and hospital monitoring.
Rapidly spreading angioedema involving the floor of the mouth or uvula threatening the airway.
High fever, joint pain, and persisting individual wheals (>24 hours) — suggests urticarial vasculitis or serum sickness-like reaction requiring urgent evaluation.

causes.cause: Allergic (IgE-mediated) reaction to food, drugs, or insect venom
causes.description: Foods (peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, fish, milk, eggs, wheat) are the most common allergic triggers in children; NSAIDs, antibiotics (penicillin, cephalosporins), and insect stings are more frequent in adults. IgE on mast cell surfaces cross-links with allergen, triggering immediate degranulation. Accounts for most acute urticaria with a clear cause.

In 35-45% of chronic spontaneous urticaria, IgG autoantibodies against the high-affinity IgE receptor (FcεRIα) or against IgE itself directly activate mast cells and basophils without requiring a specific allergen. The autologous serum skin test and basophil activation test can identify this subgroup.

Respiratory viral infections (rhinovirus, coronavirus, Epstein-Barr virus) trigger approximately 40% of acute urticaria in children via immune complex or direct mast cell activation. Chronic Helicobacter pylori infection and parasitic infections have been associated with chronic urticaria in case-control studies, though causality is debated.

NSAIDs including aspirin, ibuprofen, and naproxen provoke urticaria or angioedema in approximately 10-30% of chronic urticaria patients via a pharmacological mechanism (COX-1 inhibition redirecting arachidonic acid to leukotrienes) rather than IgE-mediated allergy. The reaction occurs with multiple structurally different NSAIDs.

Cold temperature, pressure, heat, vibration, exercise, water contact, or ultraviolet light reliably triggers wheals in chronic inducible urticaria by directly activating skin mast cells through physical pathways. Cold urticaria is the most common inducible subtype.

No triggering factor or underlying disease is identified in 50-80% of chronic spontaneous urticaria cases after systematic evaluation. The term 'idiopathic urticaria' is used when extensive workup — allergy testing, infection screen, autoimmune panel — is unrevealing.

riskFactors.description: Chronic spontaneous urticaria is twice as common in women as in men, possibly reflecting higher rates of autoimmune IgG autoantibodies against FcεRIα in women.
Peak onset of chronic spontaneous urticaria; acute urticaria has no specific age predilection. Cholinergic urticaria is most common in young adults aged 15-30.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of atopy
riskFactors.description: Atopic individuals (eczema, allergic rhinitis, asthma) have elevated IgE and more reactive mast cells. Atopy is present in 30-50% of chronic urticaria patients, higher than in the general population.

Thyroid autoimmunity (anti-TPO or anti-thyroglobulin antibodies) is found in 10-30% of chronic spontaneous urticaria patients, 3-4 times the general-population rate. The relationship may reflect a shared autoimmune tendency rather than direct causality.

Regular NSAID use provokes or worsens urticaria in 10-30% of chronic urticaria patients. Substitution with paracetamol or a selective COX-2 inhibitor (in patients tolerating these) often reduces flare frequency.

Stress worsens chronic urticaria severity in up to 40% of patients via the HPA axis and sympathetic nervous system, which activate mast cells. Stress management reduces Urticaria Activity Score (UAS7) significantly in controlled studies.

Natural salicylates, preservatives (benzoates), and artificial food colorings worsen urticaria in a subset of patients. A 3-4 week low-pseudoallergen diet improves UAS7 in approximately 30% of chronic urticaria patients in randomized trials.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 20% of the global population will experience acute urticaria in their lifetime; no significant racial predisposition.
Chronic spontaneous urticaria: female-to-male ratio approximately 2:1; peak diagnosis age 30-50 years.
Cholinergic urticaria is the most common inducible subtype in young adults aged 15-30, with a male predominance.
Cold urticaria has a younger mean age of onset (approximately 25-30 years) and is the most common form in Scandinavian and northern European populations.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis begins with a detailed history: onset, duration of individual wheals (must be under 24 hours to classify as urticaria), associated angioedema, known triggers, medication list (especially NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, hormonal therapies), infection history, dietary changes, and personal or family history of autoimmune disease. The Urticaria Activity Score over 7 days (UAS7) — ranging 0-42 — quantifies itch and wheal severity and guides treatment escalation per EAACI/GA2LEN/EDF/WAO 2022 guidelines.

For acute urticaria, a thorough history alone guides management; routine laboratory testing and allergy testing have low yield and are not recommended unless a specific allergen is suggested. For chronic urticaria, EAACI 2022 guidelines recommend a minimal baseline workup: complete blood count with differential, ESR or CRP, and thyroid function tests with TPO antibodies. Extended investigation (allergy testing, autologous serum skin test, specific IgE, infection screen for Helicobacter pylori) is reserved for patients with suggestive histories or treatment-refractory disease.

Urticarial vasculitis must be excluded when individual wheals persist beyond 24 hours, leave bruising, or accompany systemic symptoms: skin biopsy of a fresh wheal shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis rather than the perivascular mast cell infiltrate of ordinary urticaria. Cold urticaria is confirmed by an ice-cube test on the forearm with wheal development on rewarming. Dermographism is elicited by a firm stroke on the forearm. Allergy skin-prick testing and specific IgE help identify IgE-mediated triggers in acute urticaria with a suspected food or drug cause.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies urticaria severity as a validated composite of daily itch intensity (0-3) and wheal count (0-3), scored over 7 days (total 0-42); guides treatment escalation and monitors response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient-completed daily diary for 7 days; no blood draw or procedure required. A score above 28 is considered well-controlled; above 16 indicates inadequate control per EAACI guidelines.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with differential, ESR, CRP, and thyroid function/TPO antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Baseline chronic urticaria workup to exclude eosinophilia (parasitic infection), elevated inflammatory markers (vasculitis), or autoimmune thyroid disease as contributing factors
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draws. Results within 24-48 hours. TPO antibody elevation does not require treatment change unless thyroid function is abnormal, but documents autoimmune background.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms cold urticaria by applying an ice cube in a plastic bag to the forearm for 5 minutes and observing for wheal on rewarming
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in clinic, takes 15 minutes. A positive result — wheal and flare at the ice contact site within 5 minutes of rewarming — confirms cold contact urticaria. Risk of systemic reaction in severe cold urticaria; epinephrine available.

diagnosticTests.test: Allergy skin-prick testing and specific IgE to suspected allergens
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies IgE-mediated triggers in patients with acute urticaria following a specific food, drug, or insect sting exposure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed at allergy clinic; antihistamines must be stopped 5-7 days before. Read at 15 minutes. Positive result indicates sensitization; clinical relevance determined by correlation with history.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy of a wheal (hematoxylin and eosin plus immunofluorescence)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes urticarial vasculitis when wheals last over 24 hours, leave bruising, or accompany systemic symptoms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4 mm punch biopsy under local anesthetic of an active wheal present for 6-12 hours. Ordinary urticaria shows perivascular mast cell infiltrate; urticarial vasculitis shows leukocytoclastic changes. Results in 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Basophil activation test (BAT) or autologous serum skin test (ASST)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies autoimmune IgG anti-FcεRIα or anti-IgE antibodies in chronic spontaneous urticaria; not routinely required but useful in treatment-refractory cases to confirm autoimmune CSU subtype
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: ASST: serum injected intradermally; wheal response at 30 minutes is positive. BAT: flow-cytometry analysis of basophil CD63/CD203c expression; requires same-day processing in a specialized lab.

treatmentOverview:
The EAACI/GA2LEN/EDF/WAO 2022 urticaria guidelines recommend a stepwise treatment ladder. Step 1 is a licensed dose of a non-sedating second-generation H1 antihistamine (cetirizine 10 mg, bilastine 20 mg, fexofenadine 180 mg, or loratadine 10 mg daily). If inadequate control after 2-4 weeks, Step 2 escalates to up to four times the standard antihistamine dose — a robust evidence base from multiple randomized controlled trials shows improved symptom control without meaningful safety concerns.

Patients still not controlled at Step 2 (Urticaria Activity Score >6) proceed to Step 3: add omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks. Omalizumab, approved by FDA and EMA for chronic spontaneous urticaria since 2014, reduces weekly itch-severity scores by more than 50% in 65-70% of patients in the ASTERIA I and ASTERIA II trials and achieves complete remission in approximately 35-40%. Response typically manifests within 4-12 weeks.

Patients not responding to omalizumab at Step 3 may proceed to Step 4: ciclosporin (cyclosporine) 2.5-5 mg/kg/day, supported by randomized trial data and used off-label. Blood pressure and renal function require monitoring every 4 weeks.

For acute urticaria, sedating H1 antihistamines (chlorphenamine, hydroxyzine) provide faster initial relief but should be replaced by non-sedating agents for ongoing control. Short courses of oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 25-40 mg for 3-5 days) are appropriate for severe acute episodes but are not recommended for chronic urticaria due to the adverse-effect burden. NSAID avoidance and identification and elimination of triggers run concurrently with pharmacotherapy throughout all steps.

medicalTreatments.name: Second-generation H1 antihistamines (cetirizine 10-40 mg/day, bilastine 20-80 mg/day, fexofenadine 180-720 mg/day, loratadine 10-40 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for all forms of urticaria. Non-sedating; can be up-dosed to four times standard dose. Most patients require long-term daily dosing in chronic urticaria rather than as-needed use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in 50-65% of chronic urticaria patients at standard dose; 65-80% at fourfold dose in meta-analyses

medicalTreatments.name: Omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-IgE monoclonal antibody approved for chronic spontaneous urticaria refractory to antihistamines. Reduces free IgE and mast cell FcεRI receptor density. Given monthly for at least 6 months before assessing response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Weekly itch score improvement >50% in 65-70% of patients; complete remission in 35-40% in ASTERIA I and II pivotal trials (PMID 25014402 and PMID 25264308)

medicalTreatments.name: Ciclosporin (cyclosporine) 2.5-5 mg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitor suppressing mast cell and T-cell activation; used off-label as Step 4 for omalizumab-refractory chronic urticaria. Requires blood pressure and renal monitoring every 4 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 50-60% response rate in controlled trials; typically used for 3-6 months before taper

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 25-40 mg/day for 3-5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Appropriate for severe acute urticaria or acute exacerbations of chronic urticaria; not recommended long-term due to adrenal suppression, osteoporosis, and metabolic effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid symptom control in >85% of acute episodes; no benefit for long-term chronic urticaria management

medicalTreatments.name: Epinephrine auto-injector 0.3 mg IM
medicalTreatments.description: Prescribed for all patients with a history of anaphylactoid urticaria, severe angioedema, or hypotension. First-line emergency treatment for systemic reactions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reversal of anaphylaxis symptoms within 5-15 minutes in the vast majority of cases; second dose available if needed

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-pseudoallergen diet (Salicylate/benzoate-free diet)
alternativeTreatments.description: Elimination of natural and synthetic pseudoallergens — salicylates, benzoate preservatives, azo dyes — for 3-4 weeks, followed by systematic reintroduction. Randomized data show approximately 30% improvement in UAS7 scores in a subset of chronic urticaria patients; most benefit is seen in patients with confirmed NSAID hypersensitivity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin D supplementation (1,000-4,000 IU/day)
alternativeTreatments.description: Vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in chronic urticaria cohorts. One randomized trial (Rorie et al. 2014, PMID 24607050) showed supplementation significantly reduced UAS7 in deficient patients. Measuring and correcting 25-OH vitamin D is a low-risk adjunct.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist or allergist-immunologist should evaluate urticaria lasting more than 6 weeks, urticaria accompanied by angioedema, suspected inducible urticaria subtypes requiring provocation testing, or urticaria not responding to standard-dose antihistamines. Specialist assessment is needed before prescribing omalizumab and to exclude urticarial vasculitis, autoimmune disease, or rare hereditary angioedema.
doctorSelectionGuide: For chronic spontaneous urticaria, seek a board-certified dermatologist or allergist-immunologist familiar with the EAACI 2022 urticaria guidelines and with experience prescribing omalizumab. For suspected inducible urticaria, an allergy clinic with a physical challenge testing suite (ice-cube test, dermographometer, exercise protocol) is preferred. Academic medical centers with joint dermatology-allergy urticaria clinics achieve the highest rates of diagnostic precision.
hospitalCriteria: Anaphylaxis with systemic hypotension, bronchospasm, or loss of consciousness accompanying urticaria
Rapidly progressing angioedema involving the larynx or floor of the mouth
Severe exacerbation of urticaria unresponsive to IM epinephrine requiring IV epinephrine, corticosteroids, and monitoring
Initiation of omalizumab in patients with prior severe systemic reactions (first-injection observation period)
keyFacilities: Dermatology or allergy clinics with physical challenge testing (ice-cube, dermographometer, exercise provocation)
Academic urticaria centers offering omalizumab infusion suites
Emergency departments with anaphylaxis management protocols
Allergy-immunology clinics for basophil activation testing and autologous serum skin testing
preventionStrategies: Identify and consistently avoid known triggers — maintain a food, activity, and environment diary for at least 4 weeks to map individual patterns.
Avoid NSAIDs including over-the-counter ibuprofen, aspirin, and naproxen; substitute paracetamol (acetaminophen) unless contraindicated.
Wear loose, non-constrictive clothing to minimize pressure-induced urticaria; avoid tight waistbands and bra straps if pressure triggers wheals.
Use pre-cooled clothing or air-conditioned environments if heat is a trigger, and avoid hot baths or showers in heat-triggered or cholinergic urticaria.
Carry an epinephrine auto-injector if any prior episode included angioedema, throat tightness, or hypotension.
lifestyleModifications: Take prescribed antihistamines daily at the same time — consistent steady-state dosing is more effective than intermittent use for chronic urticaria.
Practice structured stress management (cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness) as validated stress reduction reduces UAS7 in controlled trials.
Apply cool water compresses or cooling gels to active wheals for immediate symptomatic relief without risk.
Avoid alcohol and spicy foods during flares, as both promote cutaneous vasodilation and mast cell mediator release.
Monitor and track UAS7 weekly using a validated app or diary to assess treatment response and inform dose-escalation decisions.
Inform all prescribers of the urticaria diagnosis to avoid NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors (which can worsen angioedema), and other histamine-releasing drugs.

recommended: Fresh, unprocessed foods with low salicylate and additive content during a trial elimination phase
Quercetin-rich foods (apples, capers, onions) with potential mast cell-stabilizing properties as a dietary adjunct
Adequate hydration — dry skin increases itch sensitivity and may worsen dermographism
Omega-3-rich oily fish (salmon, mackerel) for anti-inflammatory effects; associated with lower urticaria severity in observational data
avoid: NSAIDs and aspirin even in food-derived forms (natural salicylates in berries and tomatoes in NSAID-hypersensitive patients)
Alcohol in all forms during active flares — promotes cutaneous vasodilation and histamine release
Food preservatives (benzoates E210-E219, sulfites E220-E228) and artificial food colorings (tartrazine E102) in NSAID-hypersensitive patients
Fermented, pickled, and cured foods high in histamine during active disease

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is encouraged for general health and stress reduction. Patients with cholinergic or exercise-induced urticaria should pre-medicate with a non-sedating antihistamine 30-60 minutes before exercise and avoid exercising when hot, feverish, or after a meal. Swimming in cool (not cold) water is often tolerated better than outdoor heat exposure. Cold urticaria patients must avoid cold water swimming — cold immersion can trigger systemic anaphylaxis.
dailyManagement: Take antihistamines at the same time daily, even on symptom-free days, to maintain tissue histamine blockade.
Score itch and wheals on the UAS diary each morning and evening for tracking and clinic review.
Carry an epinephrine auto-injector if any angioedema, throat symptoms, or hypotension has previously occurred.
Avoid triggering substances (NSAIDs, alcohol, specific foods) consistently, not just during visible flares.
Apply a fragrance-free, low-irritant moisturizer daily to maintain skin-barrier function — dry skin amplifies itch.
Bring a dated written log of symptoms and potential triggers to each specialist appointment for dose-escalation or step-up decision-making.
prognosis:
The natural history of urticaria is generally favorable. Acute urticaria resolves within days to weeks in the majority of patients and does not recur in most. Chronic spontaneous urticaria follows a relapsing-remitting course: approximately 50% of patients achieve complete remission within 12 months, 65-70% within 3 years, and over 85% within 5 years without treatment. Approximately 10-15% of patients have persistent active disease beyond 5 years. Quality of life impairment is substantial during active disease — comparable to ischemic heart disease in validated studies — and justifies aggressive pharmacotherapy.

With second-generation antihistamines at up-dosed levels, 65-80% of patients achieve meaningful control. Of those requiring omalizumab, 65-70% achieve significant improvement and 35-40% complete remission. Relapse after omalizumab discontinuation is common (approximately 50-60% within 6-12 months) but re-treatment is effective. Chronic inducible urticaria tends to be more persistent than CSU; cold urticaria resolves within 5 years in only about 30% of patients. Autoimmune CSU (anti-FcεRIα positive) tends toward longer duration than idiopathic CSU.
recoveryTimeline: Acute urticaria: most episodes resolve within 24-72 hours with antihistamines; complete resolution typically within 2-6 weeks. Chronic spontaneous urticaria: 50% remit within 12 months, 85% within 5 years. Omalizumab response manifests within 4-12 weeks of first injection. Chronic inducible urticaria: variable; cold urticaria persists up to 10 years in 30-40% of patients.
complications: Anaphylaxis — life-threatening systemic mediator release occurring in approximately 30-35% of chronic urticaria patients at some point; carry epinephrine auto-injector.
Angioedema involving the larynx or tongue — rare but can be fatal without immediate epinephrine and airway management.
Sleep disturbance and psychological comorbidity — depression and anxiety occur in 30-45% of chronic urticaria patients; warrant separate evaluation and treatment.
Urticarial vasculitis — failure to diagnose urticaria-mimicking vasculitis leads to inadequate anti-inflammatory treatment and potential renal or pulmonary complications.
Significant quality-of-life impairment from ongoing itch, fatigue, and unpredictable flares — monitored with validated scores (UAS7, DLQI) and addressed with timely treatment escalation.
Drug side effects from antihistamine over-sedation — impairs driving and workplace safety; choose second-generation agents at appropriate doses.

supportResources.name: Urticaria Network e.V. (UNEV)
supportResources.description: International patient organization for urticaria and angioedema with educational materials, physician directories, and patient advocacy tools.

supportResources.name: AAAAI Patient Information: Hives (Urticaria)
supportResources.description: American Academy of Allergy, Asthma and Immunology patient-facing overview with treatment guidance and specialist finder.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists Patient Leaflet: Urticaria
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information from the UK professional dermatology body, covering acute and chronic urticaria management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Angioedema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Angioedema is deep-tissue swelling often co-occurring with urticaria. When angioedema occurs without urticaria and is non-pruritic, bradykinin-mediated causes (ACE inhibitor use, hereditary angioedema from C1-inhibitor deficiency) must be excluded — these do not respond to antihistamines or epinephrine and require specific therapies such as icatibant or C1-inhibitor concentrate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema produces fixed, chronic, scaling, and lichenified skin lesions lasting days to weeks, often in flexural areas. Hives are transient (under 24 hours), non-scaling, and migrate between sites. Both itch intensely, but the morphology, duration, and distribution are distinct.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriatic plaques are silvery-scaled, fixed, and persistent, typically on elbows, knees, and scalp. Urticarial wheals are smooth, migratory, and resolve within 24 hours. Psoriasis is not mediated by histamine and does not respond to antihistamines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hereditary Angioedema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hereditary angioedema (C1-inhibitor deficiency) causes recurrent non-pruritic subcutaneous and submucosal swelling without urticaria. It is bradykinin-mediated, does not respond to antihistamines or corticosteroids, and requires specific treatments (icatibant, berotralstat, C1-inhibitor replacement). Absent urticaria and normal C4 levels point away from ordinary urticaria.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Urticaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic urticaria is the persistent form of hives, defined by wheals recurring for over 6 weeks; the same treatment ladder applies.

relatedConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
relatedConditions.relevance: Urticaria is present in approximately 90% of anaphylactic episodes; severe urticaria with systemic symptoms meets anaphylaxis criteria and requires epinephrine.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both share IgE-mediated mast cell activation; patients with severe allergic asthma and urticaria may be candidates for omalizumab for both conditions simultaneously.

relatedConditions.name: Drug Allergy
relatedConditions.relevance: Drug reactions — particularly to NSAIDs and antibiotics — are a leading cause of acute urticaria and angioedema; allergy evaluation guides future safe drug selection.

faqs.question: What are hives and what causes them?
faqs.answer: Hives (urticaria) are raised, intensely itchy wheals that appear suddenly and resolve within 24 hours. They are caused by mast cells in the skin releasing histamine and other chemicals, triggered by allergens (foods, drugs, insect stings), viral infections, physical stimuli, or autoimmune reactions. In many cases no cause is found.

faqs.question: How long do hives last?
faqs.answer: Individual wheals resolve within 24 hours. Acute urticaria clears within 6 weeks in most patients. Chronic urticaria persists beyond 6 weeks; about 50% of patients are clear within 12 months and over 85% within 5 years. A minority experience persistent disease beyond 5 years.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for hives?
faqs.answer: Second-generation H1 antihistamines — cetirizine, fexofenadine, bilastine, or loratadine — taken daily are first-line. If standard doses fail, dosing is increased up to fourfold. Chronic hives not controlled by antihistamines are treated with omalizumab 300 mg monthly, which achieves meaningful improvement in 65-70% of patients.

faqs.question: Are hives dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most hives are uncomfortable but not dangerous. Hives become dangerous when accompanied by throat swelling, difficulty breathing, or a drop in blood pressure — signs of anaphylaxis requiring immediate epinephrine. Patients who have had these severe episodes should carry an epinephrine auto-injector at all times.

faqs.question: Can stress cause hives?
faqs.answer: Yes. Psychosocial stress worsens chronic urticaria in up to 40% of patients by activating the stress hormone system, which directly stimulates mast cells. Stress management techniques including cognitive behavioral therapy and mindfulness have been shown to reduce Urticaria Activity Score in controlled studies.

faqs.question: What foods cause hives?
faqs.answer: In IgE-mediated allergy, peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, fish, eggs, milk, and wheat are the most common food triggers. In chronic spontaneous urticaria, high-histamine foods (fermented products, alcohol, aged cheese) and food additives (benzoates, sulfites, tartrazine) worsen symptoms in a subset of patients without IgE-mediated allergy.

faqs.question: Can antihistamines cure hives permanently?
faqs.answer: Antihistamines control symptoms but do not cure the underlying cause. For most patients with chronic urticaria, antihistamines are continued until natural remission occurs — in about 50% of patients within 12 months. Stopping antihistamines before remission usually leads to symptom relapse within days.

faqs.question: What are chronic hives (chronic urticaria)?
faqs.answer: Chronic urticaria means hives occurring on most days for more than 6 weeks. The most common form is chronic spontaneous urticaria, where wheals appear without a specific trigger. It affects roughly 1% of adults and is twice as common in women. Treatment follows a stepwise EAACI guideline ladder from antihistamines to omalizumab.

faqs.question: Is omalizumab (Xolair) approved for hives?
faqs.answer: Yes. Omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks is FDA- and EMA-approved for chronic spontaneous urticaria in patients 12 years and older who remain symptomatic despite antihistamines. Pivotal trials ASTERIA I and II showed over 50% itch-score improvement in 65-70% of treated patients.

faqs.question: How are hives diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: raised wheals resolving within 24 hours, with intense pruritus. Chronic urticaria workup includes blood count, inflammatory markers, and thyroid antibodies per EAACI guidelines. Allergy skin tests are ordered only when a specific IgE-mediated trigger is suspected by history. Skin biopsy excludes urticarial vasculitis.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hives and a rash?
faqs.answer: Hives (urticaria) are transient, raised wheals that fully resolve within 24 hours without leaving a mark. Most other rashes — eczema, psoriasis, contact dermatitis — are fixed, persist for days or longer, and may scale or blister. The 24-hour resolution criterion is the key distinguishing feature of urticaria.

faqs.question: Can hives be a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: In most cases hives are self-limiting. Rarely, chronic urticaria indicates an underlying autoimmune disease (lupus, thyroid disease) or urticarial vasculitis. Wheals that persist beyond 24 hours, leave bruising, or accompany joint pain and fever warrant skin biopsy and rheumatological evaluation.

faqs.question: Do hives go away on their own?
faqs.answer: Acute hives resolve within 6 weeks without treatment in most patients. Chronic spontaneous urticaria achieves spontaneous remission in 50% within 12 months, 85% within 5 years. Chronic inducible urticaria (cold, pressure) tends to persist longer. Antihistamines speed symptom resolution and improve quality of life while waiting for natural remission.

faqs.question: What triggers cholinergic urticaria?
faqs.answer: Cholinergic urticaria is triggered by a rise in core body temperature — exercise, hot showers, emotional stress, or fever. It produces tiny 1-3 mm wheals surrounded by large erythematous flares. It affects primarily young adults and is managed with daily antihistamines, pre-exercise antihistamine dosing, and avoidance of overheating.

faqs.question: Can hives cause scarring?
faqs.answer: No. A defining feature of urticaria is that individual wheals resolve within 24 hours without scarring, bruising, or residual pigmentation. If skin changes persist after wheals clear, an alternative diagnosis such as urticarial vasculitis, erythema multiforme, or bullous pemphigoid should be considered.

faqs.question: Are hives contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Hives are not contagious. They result from the body's own mast cells releasing histamine in the skin, not from a transmissible infection. The viral infections that sometimes trigger hives in children are infectious, but the hives themselves cannot spread from person to person.

metaDescription: Hives (urticaria) are raised, itchy wheals that resolve within 24 hours, affecting 20% of people in their lifetime. Causes, diagnosis, and treatment reviewed.
